title: Pole lathe turning
subtitle: Mounting, tooling, and cutting on a reciprocating pole lathe
excerpt: The pole lathe turns a blank by a reciprocating spring-pole drive cord; the blank rotates one direction on the downstroke, reverses on the up. Cutting happens on the downstroke only. This technique covers blank mounting, roughing with the gouge, smoothing with the skew chisel, and parting off; the four operations that produce a finished turned component.
sourceNotes: English bodger tradition for pole-lathe turning of chair legs; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for reciprocating lathe method and tool positions.

content.text: The pole lathe is the oldest form of woodturning in Britain: a spring pole overhead, a drive cord looped around the blank, a foot treadle below. Press the treadle down and the cord drives the blank toward you; release the treadle and the pole springs back, reversing the blank. Cutting happens on the downstroke only; the tool cuts, the blank reverses, the tool waits, the blank comes around again. Working on
green wood
is essential for a pole lathe: the treadle force is enough to cut green wood cleanly, but seasoned hardwood requires more torque than a pole can deliver.
Mounting the blank

The blank must be centred between the poppets (the two uprights that hold the rotating centres) and the drive cord must be wrapped in a single tight loop around the centre of the blank. Mark the end-grain centres with a pencil cross before mounting. Drive the fixed centre (the right poppet) into the end grain with a mallet tap; advance the left poppet until its centre engages the opposite end. The blank should rotate without wobble; a wobbly blank indicates an off-centre mounting. Correct by withdrawing one centre, adjusting the blank position, and re-engaging.
Roughing with the gouge
The No. 7 sweep roughing gouge in 20 mm width is the first tool on the blank. The
gouge sweep

is curved enough to take a wide shaving on the downstroke without the corners digging in. Rest the gouge on the tool rest with the bevel rubbing the work and the handle below the lathe bed; this is the critical position. Press the treadle, allow the blank to come around to the downstroke, then advance the gouge into the wood by lifting the handle fractionally. The shaving comes off with each treadle press. Work from one end to the other in overlapping passes, bringing the blank from a rough polygon to a cylinder. The target for roughing is a cylinder within 3 mm of the intended finished diameter.
Smoothing with the skew chisel
The
skew chisel

removes the gouge marks and brings the cylinder to its finished diameter. Rest the chisel on the tool rest with the long point of the skew facing away from you and the bevel flat on the cylinder. Use only the middle third of the blade; the long point digs in if engaged, and the short point leaves a spiral mark. Draw the chisel along the cylinder in a single pass on each treadle downstroke. The result is a smooth, shear-cut surface.
Parting off

A firmer chisel driven into the spinning blank at the parting point makes the final cut. Hold the chisel at 90 degrees to the cylinder, bevel toward the work, and push it in on the downstroke. It cuts a parting groove down to the centre. Stop 5 mm from the centre and finish the cut with a handsaw or a knife while the blank is still. If you part it right through with the chisel while it spins, the blank splits in a way you cannot predict.
Common mistakes
Cutting on the upstroke: the blank is rotating away from the tool on the upstroke and the tool will catch rather than cut. Only cut on the downstroke; wait for the blank to come around.

Tool rest too far from the blank: the further the tool rests from the work, the more leverage the cut forces exert on the handle. Keep the rest within 5 mm of the cylinder and re-set it as the diameter reduces.

Using seasoned wood: seasoned hardwood requires more torque than a foot treadle can deliver. Use green wood only; a blank that has dried for more than a week will resist the gouge and cause the cord to slip on the cylinder.

Long point of the skew chisel engaging the work: the long point digs in and the chisel catches, sometimes violently. Use only the middle third of the blade and keep the long point clear of the surface.
